items.q: Does Brandenburg Concerto No. 5 feature a piano solo?
items.correct: No - the keyboard soloist plays the harpsichord, the standard keyboard instrument of Bach's time.
items.misconception: The brilliant keyboard part in Brandenburg No. 5 is written for piano.
items.random_wrong: The keyboard part is played on a church organ with pedals.
items.why: The modern piano had not displaced the harpsichord in the 1720s; recognising period instruments is essential for describing Baroque sonority correctly.

items.q: Does the slow Grave introduction of the 'Pathetique' disappear after the opening?
items.correct: No - its material returns during the movement, dramatically interrupting the fast music.
items.misconception: The introduction is heard once and never comes back.
items.random_wrong: The Grave is repeated unchanged at the start of every bar.
items.why: Beethoven integrates the introduction into the sonata-form argument; spotting its returns is a key analytical point about this set work.

items.q: Are Baroque and Classical just two names for the same style?
items.correct: No - Baroque music features continuo, ornamented lines and terraced dynamics, while Classical style prefers balanced phrases, clearer textures and graded dynamics.
items.misconception: Music from 1700 and music from 1800 sound essentially identical.
items.random_wrong: The only difference is that Classical pieces are always louder.
items.why: This area of study spans both styles deliberately; comparing Bach's and Beethoven's idioms requires hearing the stylistic shift around 1750.

items.q: Which three solo instruments feature in Bach's Brandenburg Concerto No. 5?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Flute, violin and harpsichord. “Oboe, horn and organ” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Beethoven's 'Pathetique' Sonata is in which key?
items.why: It's C minor. “C major” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In a concerto grosso, the concertino is what?
items.why: It's The small group of soloists. “The full string ensemble” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What is the role of the basso continuo?
items.correct: To provide the bass line with improvised chords above it
items.why: The answer examiners want is: To provide the bass line with improvised chords above it. “To play the main melody” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: The 'Pathetique' is Beethoven's piano sonata number what?
items.why: The real figure is 8 sonata number. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
